unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Who This Is For ESL students aged 5–7 years. Absolute beginners with limited or no knowledge of the English alphabet. Children preparing to enter Cambridge Global English Year 1. What Children Will Be Able to Do
unit.learningPath:
Week 1: meet uppercase and lowercase letters.
Weeks 2-3: connect letters with sounds.
Week 4: blend simple CVC words.
Week 5: read first sight words and tiny sentences.
Week 6: review, celebrate and complete a gentle readiness check.

visual.alt: Young learners exploring alphabet letters and picture cues

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A round fruit that is sweet and crunchy. It can be red or green.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I eat an apple after school.
practiceSentences: Musa has a red apple.
Is the apple red or green?
Yes, please, I want an apple!
My apple tastes sweet and juicy.
An apple grows on a tall tree.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: a - p - p - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say apple slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A round toy that you can throw, kick and bounce.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Yusuf kicks the ball to me.
practiceSentences: Aisha throws the ball to Musa.
Can you catch this big ball?
Yes, I can bounce the ball!
My ball is blue and white.
The ball rolls under the table.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: b - a - l - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say ball slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A soft pet animal that says miaow and has long whiskers.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The cat sleeps on my bed.
practiceSentences: A little cat sits by the door.
Whose cat is this, Samira?
That grey cat is mine!
My cat likes warm milk.
The cat runs after a small bug.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: c - a - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say cat slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A pet animal that barks, wags its tail and likes long walks.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The dog barks at the gate.
practiceSentences: Ishmael sees a big brown dog.
Is that dog friendly?
Yes, the dog is very kind!
My dog runs in the garden.
The dog sleeps beside its bowl.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: d - o - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say dog slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very big grey animal with a long trunk and huge ears.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: An elephant drinks with its trunk.
practiceSentences: An elephant is very big and grey.
Have you seen an elephant before?
Yes, I saw an elephant at the zoo!
My favourite animal is the elephant.
The elephant walks slowly to the water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Clap the parts, then copy: elephant
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say elephant slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An animal that lives in water and swims with its tail.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The fish swims in clear water.
practiceSentences: A little fish swims in the pond.
Can you see the orange fish?
Yes, the fish is hiding!
My fish lives in a glass tank.
We eat fish and rice for dinner.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: f - i - s - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say fish slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A farm animal with horns that gives us milk.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The goat eats green grass.
practiceSentences: The goat eats grass on the hill.
Does the goat have small horns?
Look, the goat is running!
My uncle keeps one white goat.
A baby goat stays near its mother.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: g - o - a - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say goat slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something you wear on your head to keep warm or shady.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Musa wears a warm hat.
practiceSentences: Musa puts on his blue hat.
Where is my sun hat?
Here is your hat, Samira.
My hat keeps my head warm.
The hat hangs on a hook.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: h - a - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say hat slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A little round house made from blocks of ice and snow.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: An igloo is made of snow.
practiceSentences: An igloo is built from snow blocks.
Is it cold inside an igloo?
Yes, an igloo feels very cold!
I can draw an igloo.
The igloo has a small round door.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: i - g - l - o - o
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say igloo slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Sweet, sticky food made from fruit. We spread it on bread.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I spread jam on my bread.
practiceSentences: Aisha puts jam on her toast.
Do you like strawberry jam?
Yes, jam is my favourite!
My jam is sweet and red.
The jam jar is on the shelf.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: j - a - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say jam slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A light toy that flies high in the sky on a string.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My kite flies high in the sky.
practiceSentences: Yusuf flies his kite in the park.
Can your kite fly very high?
Look, the kite is going up!
My kite is red and yellow.
The kite dances in the wind.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: k - i - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say kite slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big wild cat with a loud roar and golden fur.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The lion has a big mane.
practiceSentences: The lion roars in the hot sun.
Is the lion sleeping now?
Yes, the lion is resting!
I like the strong lion best.
A lion lives in the tall grass.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: l - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say lion slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The bright light we see in the night sky.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The moon shines at night.
practiceSentences: The moon is bright tonight.
Can you see the moon, Musa?
Yes, the moon looks round!
I watch the moon from my window.
We look for the new moon before Eid.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: m - o - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say moon slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A little home birds make from sticks for their eggs.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A bird sits in the nest.
practiceSentences: The bird makes a soft nest.
What is inside the nest?
Three little eggs are in the nest!
I found a nest in our tree.
The nest is high above the wall.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: n - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say nest slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A round juicy fruit with a thick orange skin.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Samira peels a juicy orange.
practiceSentences: Samira peels a juicy orange for me.
May I have an orange, please?
Yes, take this sweet orange.
My orange tastes lovely and sweet.
An orange grows on a green tree.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: o - r - a - n - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say orange slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A thin tool with ink that we write with.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I write my name with a pen.
practiceSentences: Teacher writes with a black pen.
Where is my new pen?
Your pen is on the desk.
My pen writes in blue ink.
The pen rolls off the table.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: p - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say pen slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A woman who rules a country and wears a crown.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The queen wears a golden crown.
practiceSentences: The queen lives in a big palace.
Does the queen wear a crown?
Yes, the queen has a gold crown!
I drew a queen in my book.
The queen waves to the children.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: q - u - e - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say queen slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Curved bands of colour in the sky after rain and sun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A rainbow shines after the rain.
practiceSentences: A bright rainbow is in the sky.
How many colours has the rainbow?
Look, Samira, a rainbow!
I painted a rainbow at school.
The rainbow comes after the rain stops.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: r - a - i - n - b - o - w
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say rainbow slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The big hot star that gives us light in the day.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sun is warm today.
practiceSentences: The sun shines on our school.
Is the sun hot today?
Yes, the sun is very warm!
I wear my hat in the sun.
The sun goes down in the evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: s - u - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say sun slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A tall plant with a wooden trunk, branches and green leaves.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The tree gives us cool shade.
practiceSentences: A tall tree grows near the mosque.
Can you climb that tree?
No, the tree is too tall!
I sit under the tree at break.
Birds make a nest in the tree.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: t - r - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say tree slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something you hold over your head to stay dry in rain.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I open my umbrella in the rain.
practiceSentences: Musa holds a big umbrella.
May I share your umbrella?
Yes, come under my umbrella!
My umbrella is blue and green.
The umbrella keeps the rain off us.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Clap the parts, then copy: umbrella
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say umbrella slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big car with lots of room for boxes or people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The van takes us to school.
practiceSentences: A white van stops at our gate.
Is the school van here yet?
Yes, the van is waiting!
I ride in the van every morning.
The van carries big boxes inside.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: v - a - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say van slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A clear drink with no colour. We need it every day.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I drink water when I am thirsty.
practiceSentences: Samira drinks cool water after playing.
May I have some water, please?
Here is your water, Yusuf.
I wash my hands with water.
The fish swim in the water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: w - a - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say water slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A container with straight sides for keeping things inside.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My toys are in the box.
practiceSentences: The crayons are in a small box.
What is inside that box?
Open the box and see!
I keep my socks in a box.
The box is too heavy to lift.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: b - o - x
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say box slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A round toy on a string that rolls down and up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My yo-yo goes up and down.
practiceSentences: Ishmael plays with his yo-yo.
Can you use a yo-yo?
Watch my yo-yo go down!
My yo-yo is red and shiny.
The yo-yo spins on its string.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: y - o - - - y - o
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say yo-yo slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A wild animal like a horse with black and white stripes.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A zebra has black and white stripes.
practiceSentences: The zebra has many black stripes.
Where does a zebra live?
A zebra lives where the grass is dry!
I like the zebra in my book.
The zebra runs fast across the grass.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: z - e - b - r - a
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say zebra slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word you use when you talk about yourself.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I am six years old.
practiceSentences: I like reading with my teacher.
May I sit here, please?
Yes, I can help you!
I say bismillah before I eat.
Every morning I walk to school.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: I
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say I slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word we put before a naming word, like 'the cat'.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The cat sits on the mat.
practiceSentences: The teacher opens the book.
Where is the pencil?
The pencil is here, Musa!
I read the story to my sister.
The children sit on the mat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: t - h - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say the slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word we use before one thing, like 'a hat'.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I have a red hat.
practiceSentences: Musa has a big blue ball.
Would you like a pencil?
Yes, please give me a pencil.
I drew a cat in my book.
We sit on a soft mat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: a
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say a slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word that tells us about now, like 'she is happy'.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My sister is very kind.
practiceSentences: The sun is bright today.
Where is your lunchbox?
It is under the table!
My name is Samira.
This book is about a goat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: i - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say is slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To look at something and know it with your eyes.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I see the moon at night.
practiceSentences: We see many birds in the tree.
What can you see, Yusuf?
I see two green frogs!
I see my friend at the gate.
Owls can see well at night.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: s - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say see slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word that shows something belongs to you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My bag is on the chair.
practiceSentences: My teacher reads us a story.
Have you seen my pencil?
Here is my pencil, thank you.
I love my mum and dad.
My shoes are by the door.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: m - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say my slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word that means you are able to do something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can write my name.
practiceSentences: Aisha can jump very high.
Can you tie your shoes?
Yes, I can do it!
I can read this little book.
Birds can fly in the sky.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: c - a - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say can slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word we use instead of naming a thing again.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is my favourite red ball.
practiceSentences: It is raining outside today.
Where did you put it?
I put it in my bag.
My cat is small and it purrs.
It is time for our lesson.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: i - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say it slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small word for you and other people together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We read a book together.
practiceSentences: We line up at the door.
Can we play outside now?
Yes, we can go outside!
We eat lunch with my family.
On Eid we visit our grandma.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: w - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say we slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To think something is nice and enjoy it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I like sweet mangoes.
practiceSentences: Musa and Aisha like painting.
Do you like this story?
Yes, I like it very much!
I like helping my mum at home.
Cats like to sleep in the sun.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: l - i - k - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say like slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Learner can recognise and name uppercase and lowercase letters.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, matching, saying, tracing, blending and a gentle one-to-one check.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Learner can connect letters a-z with their most common sounds.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, matching, saying, tracing, blending and a gentle one-to-one check.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Learner can hear and say beginning sounds in familiar words.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, matching, saying, tracing, blending and a gentle one-to-one check.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Learner can blend and read simple CVC words.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, matching, saying, tracing, blending and a gentle one-to-one check.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Learner can recognise ten first sight words.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, matching, saying, tracing, blending and a gentle one-to-one check.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Learner can build and read a tiny patterned sentence.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, matching, saying, tracing, blending and a gentle one-to-one check.

readings.title: Week 1: Learn the Alphabet (A–Z)
readings.theme: letters, sounds, blending and first sight words
readings.setting: Grade 1 classroom or supported home lesson
readings.passageScript:
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 1, children will begin to:
Recognise all 26 letters of the English alphabet.
Identify and name letters in both uppercase and lowercase forms.
Trace and begin to form basic letter shapes.
Associate each letter with a simple, friendly picture cue.
Join in alphabet songs, chants and movement with confidence and joy.
The Five Daily Focuses
Day 1 — Letters A–E: meeting the alphabet. Apple, Ball, Cat, Dog, Elephant.
Day 2 — Letters F–J: review A–E. Fish, Goat, Hat, Igloo, Jam.
Day 3 — Letters K–O: review A–J. Kite, Lion, Moon, Nest, Orange.
Day 4 — Letters P–T: review A–O. Pen, Queen, Rainbow, Sun, Tree.
Day 5 — Letters U–Z: review A–T, then celebrate the whole alphabet! Umbrella, Van, Water, Box (–x), Yo-yo, Zebra.
Materials to Prepare
A large A–Z alphabet chart or wall strip (printable).
Letter flashcards — uppercase and lowercase for all 26 letters.

readings.title: Week 2: Phonics (Letter Sounds a–m)
readings.theme: letters, sounds, blending and first sight words
readings.setting: Grade 1 classroom or supported home lesson
readings.passageScript:
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Guide
Why sounds this week?
In Week 1 the children met the letters as shapes and names (“This is A, this is B”). This week we add the next layer: every letter also makes a sound. Hearing and copying these sounds is the very first step towards reading. Keep the mood light and warm — for a five- to six-year-old, joyfully copying /m/ while rubbing their tummy is a real success, even if they cannot yet name the letter every time.
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 2, children will be able to (with support):
Hear and copy the sounds for letters a–m.
Begin to match a letter to the sound it makes, and a sound back to its letter.
Notice the first sound in simple, familiar words (e.g. hear the /m/ at the start of “moon”).
Tell some sounds apart from one another when they are said aloud.
Understand the big idea that letters stand for sounds in the words we say and read.
Daily Sound Focus
Day 1 — a, b, c: /a/ as in *apple*, /b/ as in *ball*, /k/ as in *cat*. Introducing the idea that letters make sounds.
Day 2 — d, e, f: /d/ as in *dog*, /e/ as in *egg*, /f/ as in *fish*. Noticing the mouth and beginning sounds.
Day 3 — g, h, i: /g/ as in *goat*, /h/ as in *hat*, /i/ as in *igloo*. Telling sounds apart (discrimination).
Day 4 — j, k, l: /j/ as in *jam*, /k/ as in *kite*, /l/ as in *lion*. Sorting and beginning-sound games (k and c share /k/).
Day 5 — m and review: /m/ as in *moon*, then a friendly review and check of all sounds a–m.
Materials for the Week

readings.title: Week 3: Phonics (N–Z)
readings.theme: letters, sounds, blending and first sight words
readings.setting: Grade 1 classroom or supported home lesson
readings.passageScript:
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 3, children will be able to (with support and lots of repetition):
Recognise and say the sounds for letters n through z.
Match these letters to their sounds using pictures and actions.
Hear the beginning sound in simple, familiar words.
Tell apart sounds that are easy to mix up, such as /p/ and /b/, /t/ and /d/, /s/ and /z/.
Join up their whole-week learning to know all 26 letter sounds (a–z) in a fun, low-pressure way.
Daily Sound Focus
Day 1 — Sounds n, o, p
/n/ as in nest, /ɒ/ as in orange, /p/ as in pen.
Notice how /p/ is a strong puff of air, close to /b/ from last week.
Day 2 — Sounds q, r, s
/kw/ as in queen, /r/ as in rainbow, /s/ as in sun.
Notice how q is special — it makes two sounds together and almost always brings a u with it.
Day 3 — Sounds t, u, v
/t/ as in tree, /ʌ/ as in umbrella, /v/ as in van.

readings.title: Week 4: Blending Sounds (CVC Words)
readings.theme: letters, sounds, blending and first sight words
readings.setting: Grade 1 classroom or supported home lesson
readings.passageScript:
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 4, children will be able to:
Segment a simple CVC word into its three separate sounds (for example, hear /s/ /u/ /n/ in “sun”).
Blend three sounds smoothly back together to say a whole word.
Read simple CVC words with all five short vowels: a, e, i, o, u.
Match a CVC word to the picture it names.
Begin to feel the joy of “word building” - changing one sound to make a new word (cat → hat → hut).
The Five Daily Focuses
Day 1 – Introduction to Sound Blending: review letter sounds; meet the idea of pushing sounds together; segment words and blend the first two sounds (consonant + vowel).
Day 2 – Short a CVC Words: blend and read full words such as cat, hat, map, bat, fan.
Day 3 – Short e and i CVC Words: blend and read bed, pen, leg and pin, big, sit.
Day 4 – Short o and u CVC Words: blend and read dog, top, pot and sun, cup, bug.
Day 5 – Mixed CVC Practice & Check-in: blend words with all five vowels, word-building challenges, and a gentle one-to-one reading check.
Materials for the Week
Low-cost and printable:
Letter cards for every sound learned so far (paper or card).

readings.title: Week 5: First Sight Words + Alphabet Practice
readings.theme: letters, sounds, blending and first sight words
readings.setting: Grade 1 classroom or supported home lesson
readings.passageScript:
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan
What Sight Words Are (a quick note for the teacher)
Sight words are high-frequency words that appear again and again in English. Many cannot be easily sounded out (“the,” “you”), so children learn to recognise them as whole shapes, the way they recognise a friend's face. We teach them by seeing, saying, moving and playing — not by blending sounds. This sits happily beside the phonics work the children already know.
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 5, children will be able to:
Recognise and read 12–15 high-frequency sight words on sight.
Blend a short CVC word with a sight word to read a tiny sentence (e.g. I see a cat.).
Keep recognising letter names and their sounds from earlier weeks.
Begin to feel what a sentence is — several words that go together and start with a capital.
Feel proud and confident as early readers.
The Five Daily Focuses
Day 1 — Meet sight words: review alphabet & CVC, then learn I, a, the.
Day 2 — More words + alphabet: review, learn and, is, to, play word games.
Day 3 — Words in sentences: learn in, it, you, and build first simple sentences.
Day 4 — More words + phonics: learn for, my, we, revisit tricky sounds.
Day 5 — Review & celebrate: learn see, can, go, gentle check-in, certificates.
Materials for the Week

readings.title: Week 6: Alphabet & Sounds Review
readings.theme: letters, sounds, blending and first sight words
readings.setting: Grade 1 classroom or supported home lesson
readings.passageScript:
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Weekly Overview
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 6, children will be able to:
Recognise all 26 letters of the alphabet, uppercase and lowercase.
Match each letter to its sound (letter-sound correspondence).
Blend simple CVC words (like *cat, sun, big*) with growing confidence.
Recognise and read the 15 sight words taught in Week 5.
Show they are ready for Cambridge Global English Year 1 — settled, confident, and curious.
Daily Focus
Day 1 — Alphabet Review
Revisit all 26 letters (uppercase and lowercase) through games and songs.
Practise letter formation and celebrate recognition since Week 1.
Day 2 — Phonics Review
Revisit every letter sound and practise telling similar sounds apart (p/b, t/d).
Hear and name beginning sounds in familiar words.
Day 3 — Blending Skills Review
Push sounds together to read CVC words with all five short vowels.

comprehension.question: Point to Cat on the Day 1 list. Say its letter.
comprehension.explanation: Day 1 gives Cat as the picture cue for the letter C.

comprehension.question: Day 5 uses Zebra. Show that letter and say its sound.
comprehension.explanation: Day 5 covers letters U-Z and gives Zebra as the picture cue for Z.

comprehension.question: Day 5 teaches the sound /m/. Say the plan's word for /m/.
comprehension.explanation: The Day 5 sound focus is /m/ as in moon.

comprehension.question: Which two letters this week share the /k/ sound? Say both.
comprehension.explanation: Day 4 says that k and c share the /k/ sound.

comprehension.question: Day 1 gives nest, orange and pen. Say the sound for nest.
The plan says q is special. Which letter almost always comes with q?
comprehension.explanation: Day 2 says q almost always brings a u with it.

comprehension.question: Blend these sounds from the plan: /s/ /u/ /n/. Say the word.
comprehension.explanation: The objectives use sun as the example of blending /s/ /u/ /n/.

comprehension.question: The plan changes cat to hat. Which word comes next?
comprehension.explanation: The word-building example in the objectives is cat, then hat, then hut.

comprehension.section: Week 5: First Sight Words + Alphabet Practice
comprehension.question: Day 1 teaches three sight words. Say all three.
comprehension.explanation: Day 1 says: review alphabet and CVC, then learn I, a, the.

comprehension.section: Week 5: First Sight Words + Alphabet Practice
comprehension.question: Read the tiny sentence in the plan about a cat.
comprehension.explanation: The objectives show 'I see a cat.' as the first tiny sentence.

comprehension.question: Day 2 tells similar sounds apart. Say the pair that begins with p.
comprehension.explanation: Day 2 says to practise telling p/b and t/d apart.

comprehension.question: How many Week 5 sight words can you read by Week 6?
comprehension.explanation: The Week 6 objectives say children read the 15 sight words taught in Week 5.

grammar.title: The letter A says /a/
grammar.explanation: Say the short sound /a/, not the letter name. Open your mouth wide, like Musa saying a-a-apple.
grammar.commonMistake: Many children say the letter name 'ay' instead of the sound /a/. Model the short /a/ first, then let them copy.
grammar.memoryTip: Open your mouth wide and pat your tummy: a, a, apple!
grammar.practice: Say /a/ three times, then name three things that start with it: ant, apple, arm.

grammar.title: The letter M says /m/
grammar.explanation: Press your lips together and hum. Keep the sound going: mmmm, like warm food cooking at home.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children add an extra sound and say 'muh'. Keep the lips shut and hum /m/ with no 'uh' at the end.
grammar.memoryTip: Rub your tummy and hum mmmm, like Mum's warm milk.
grammar.practice: Hum /m/ slowly, then say three /m/ words: mum, mat, moon.

grammar.title: The letter S says /s/
grammar.explanation: Put your teeth together and let the air hiss out: ssss. That is the sound at the start of sun.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often say 'ess', the letter name. Hiss the sound instead, long and soft like a snake.
grammar.memoryTip: Wiggle your hand like a snake while you hiss ssss.
grammar.practice: Hiss /s/ for three seconds, then say three /s/ words: sun, sit, sand.

grammar.title: Blend three sounds: c-a-t
grammar.explanation: Say each sound on its own, then push them together quickly to make the whole word: c-a-t, cat.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say the letters' names (see-ay-tee) instead of their sounds (c-a-t). Say the sounds slowly, then blend.
grammar.memoryTip: Tap one finger for each sound, then sweep your hand across to say the word.
grammar.practice: Blend and say three words: c-a-t cat, m-a-t mat, s-a-t sat.

grammar.title: Say what you can see
grammar.explanation: Look around and name one thing. Use 'a' before most words, but 'an' before an a, e, i, o, u sound: a bag, an apple.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before apple by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an apple, an egg. Say it slowly together.
grammar.memoryTip: If the word starts with a wide-open mouth sound — a, e, i, o, u — use 'an'.
grammar.practice: Look around and say three: I see a book. I see an apple. I see a bag.

grammar.title: Tell people what you can do
grammar.explanation: Think of something you are able to do, then say it out loud. Use one action word after 'can'.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'I can running.' After 'can' we use the plain action word: 'I can run.'
grammar.memoryTip: Flex your arm strongly each time you say 'I can'.
grammar.practice: Say three about yourself: I can run. I can jump. I can read.

speaking.title: Sound and say 1
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Say the /a/ sound five times, then find things that begin with it. Say: 'A says /a/.' 'A is for apple.' 'A is for Aisha.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, model one sound or tiny sentence slowly, then wait for the child to repeat.

speaking.title: Sound and say 2
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Rub your tummy and hum /m/ like tasty food. Say: 'M says /m/.' 'M is for mum.' 'M is for Musa.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, model one sound or tiny sentence slowly, then wait for the child to repeat.

speaking.title: Sound and say 3
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Hiss like a snake for the /s/ sound. Say: 'S says /s/.' 'S is for sun.' 'S is for Samira.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, model one sound or tiny sentence slowly, then wait for the child to repeat.

speaking.title: Sound and say 4
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Be a robot. Say each sound, then push the sounds together. Say: 'c-a-t, cat.' 'm-a-t, mat.' 'p-i-n, pin.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, model one sound or tiny sentence slowly, then wait for the child to repeat.

speaking.title: Sound and say 5
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Look around the room and name three things you can see. Say: 'I see a book.' 'I see a mat.' 'I see a cup.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, model one sound or tiny sentence slowly, then wait for the child to repeat.

speaking.title: Sound and say 6
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Show an action, then tell an adult about it. Say: 'I can hop.' 'I can clap.' 'I can say my letters.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, model one sound or tiny sentence slowly, then wait for the child to repeat.

writing.title: Trace uppercase letters
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace five big letters: A, B, M, S, T. Use your finger first, then a crayon. Say each letter name as you trace it.
writing.successCriteria: I say the sound or word; start in the right place; follow the shape; read back what I made
writing.support: Finger tracing, sand tray, large print, letter cards and adult modelling.
writing.extension: Find another letter, word or picture with the same sound.

writing.title: Trace lowercase letters
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the small letters a, m, s, t, p. Say the sound each time, not the letter name: /a/, /m/, /s/, /t/, /p/.
writing.successCriteria: I say the sound or word; start in the right place; follow the shape; read back what I made
writing.support: Finger tracing, sand tray, large print, letter cards and adult modelling.
writing.extension: Find another letter, word or picture with the same sound.

writing.title: Match capital and small letters
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw a line to join each big letter to its small partner: A-a, M-m, S-s, T-t, P-p. Say both letters as you join them.
writing.successCriteria: I say the sound or word; start in the right place; follow the shape; read back what I made
writing.support: Finger tracing, sand tray, large print, letter cards and adult modelling.
writing.extension: Find another letter, word or picture with the same sound.

writing.title: Build a CVC word
writing.promptAndInstructions: Use letter cards to build cat, mat and sat. Sound out each word, then copy your favourite one neatly on the line.
writing.expectedLength: One word, phrase or tiny sentence
writing.successCriteria: I say the sound or word; start in the right place; follow the shape; read back what I made
writing.support: Finger tracing, sand tray, large print, letter cards and adult modelling.
writing.extension: Find another letter, word or picture with the same sound.

writing.title: Copy a sight-word phrase
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace 'I see a', then copy it once on your own. Finish it with one word you can really see: sun, bag or cat.
writing.expectedLength: One word, phrase or tiny sentence
writing.successCriteria: I say the sound or word; start in the right place; follow the shape; read back what I made
writing.support: Finger tracing, sand tray, large print, letter cards and adult modelling.
writing.extension: Find another letter, word or picture with the same sound.

writing.title: Write a tiny sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Pick a picture of something you can do. Write 'I can' and add one word: hop, sit or run. Read your sentence to an adult.
writing.expectedLength: One word, phrase or tiny sentence
writing.successCriteria: I say the sound or word; start in the right place; follow the shape; read back what I made
writing.support: Finger tracing, sand tray, large print, letter cards and adult modelling.
writing.extension: Find another letter, word or picture with the same sound.

activities.title: Alphabet hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems: Hunt for letters around you. Find: 1. an A on a book, 2. an M on a food packet, 3. an S on a sign, 4. the first letter of your name. Name each one.
activities.answerSummary: Success is four letters found and named: an A on a book, an M on a packet, an S on a sign, and the first letter of their own name. Accept pointing plus either the letter name or its sound.

activities.title: Sound sorting
activities.instructionsAndItems: Make two piles: /s/ and /m/. Sort these picture cards - sun, sock, mat, moon, milk, seed. Say each word slowly before you place it.
activities.answerSummary: The /s/ pile is sun, sock and seed; the /m/ pile is mat, moon and milk. Accept a correct sort even when the word is stretched out slowly with your help or the sound is whispered.

activities.title: Picture match
activities.instructionsAndItems: Lay out three sets: A-a-apple, T-t-tap, P-p-pen. Mix them up, then match big letter, small letter and picture. Say the sound each time.
activities.answerSummary: The three complete sets are A-a-apple, T-t-tap and P-p-pen. Accept a child who matches by picture first and then finds the letters, and count /a/, /t/ or /p/ as right if roughly formed.

activities.title: Robot blending
activities.instructionsAndItems: Play robot talk. An adult says the sounds and you blend the word: s-u-n, m-a-p, t-i-n, b-a-g. Then be the robot yourself.
activities.answerSummary: Listen for sun, map, tin and bag blended into whole words. Accept a slow sound-by-sound blend, one prompt from you before the word arrives, and any brave robot voice when they take their own turn.

activities.title: Sight-word snap
activities.instructionsAndItems: Turn over cards for I, see, a and can. Snap when two match, then read the word aloud. The player with the most pairs wins.
activities.answerSummary: Look for snapping only on true pairs of I, see, a and can, followed by reading the word aloud. Accept a slow sound-out or the word echoed after you; who wins matters far less than the reading.

activities.title: Tiny sentence builder
activities.instructionsAndItems: Spread out word cards: I, can, see, a, cat, sun. Build one tiny sentence, read it aloud, then swap the last card and read it again.
activities.answerSummary: A correct build is any sensible order, such as 'I can see a cat', read left to right. Accept finger-pointing while reading, and 'I can see a sun' after the last card is swapped.

assignments.title: My Alphabet and Sounds mini-book
assignments.instructions: Make a six-page alphabet mini-book. 1) Fold three sheets of paper together. 2) On each page write one favourite letter and draw its picture word, such as apple, moon or zebra. 3) Add three sounding-out words: cat, sun, pen. 4) Write five sight words: I, the, a, my, can. 5) Read your book aloud with a grown-up, blending c-a-t slowly, and finish with one sentence: I can see a ___.

quizzes.question: Which capital letter matches the small letter a?
quizzes.explanation: Small a is written as capital A; B, D and O are the partners of b, d and o.

quizzes.question: Which small letter matches the capital letter M?
quizzes.explanation: Capital M is written as small m; w is m turned over, and n and s are different letters.

quizzes.question: Which word begins with the /b/ sound?
quizzes.explanation: Ball starts with b, so we hear /b/; cat starts with /c/, sun with /s/ and fish with /f/.

quizzes.question: Which word begins with the /s/ sound?
quizzes.explanation: Sun starts with s, so we hear /s/; moon, dog and hat begin with /m/, /d/ and /h/.

quizzes.question: Blend these sounds: c - a - t. Which word do you get?
quizzes.explanation: The middle sound is /a/ and the last sound is /t/, so c-a-t says cat, not cap or can.

quizzes.question: Blend these sounds: s - u - n. Which word do you get?
quizzes.explanation: The last sound is /n/, so s-u-n says sun; sum ends with /m/ and run begins with /r/.

quizzes.question: Which word completes the sentence: ___ cat sat on the mat.
quizzes.explanation: The is our sight word and it goes before cat; the other words are already used in the sentence.

quizzes.question: Which word completes the sentence: I ___ a cat.
quizzes.explanation: See is a doing word that fits after I; moon, hat and pen are naming words, so they do not work here.

quizzes.question: Which sentence begins with a capital letter?
quizzes.options: I see a cat. | i see a cat. | see a cat. | cat I see
quizzes.explanation: Only this one starts with the capital letter I; the others start with small i, s and c.

quizzes.question: Which sentence ends with a full stop?
quizzes.options: can hop I | I can hop. | I can hop | hop
quizzes.explanation: Only this one has the little dot at the end; the others have no full stop.

liveSessions.title: Day 1: Letters A–E
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today’s lesson, children will begin to:; Meet the idea of the alphabet — that English is written with 26 special letters.; Recognise and name letters A, B, C, D, E in both uppercase (BIG) and lowercase (small) forms.; Match each new letter to a friendly picture (for example, “A is for Apple”).
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 1: Letter Sounds a, b, c
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; Warmly review the names and shapes of a, b, c from Week 1.; Discover the big idea that letters make sounds.; Hear and copy the sounds a (/a/ as in *apple*), b (/b/ as in *ball*) and c (/k/ as in *cat*).; Begin to hear these sounds at the start of words.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 2: Letter Sounds d, e, f

liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; Cheerfully review the sounds a, b, c with their actions.; Hear and copy the sounds d (/d/ as in *dog*), e (/e/ as in *egg*) and f (/f/ as in *fish*).; Begin to hear these sounds at the start of words.; Notice how the mouth changes for each sound.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.

liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; Warmly review the sounds a–m learned in Week 2.; Learn the sounds n (/n/), o (/ɒ/ as in orange), and p (/p/).; Hear these sounds at the beginning of simple words.; Link each sound to a mouth shape and a fun hand-action.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; Warmly review the sounds a–p (all of Week 2 plus yesterday's n, o, p).; Learn the sounds q (/kw/), r (/r/), and s (/s/).; Hear these sounds at the beginning of simple words.; Link each sound to a mouth shape and a fun hand-action.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 5: Sound z and Full Review

liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; Warmly review the sounds a–y learned so far.; Learn the final sound z (/z/).; Practise ALL of this week's new sounds (n–z).; Join everything up to recognise all 26 letter sounds (a–z).
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 1: Introduction to Sound Blending – pushing sounds together
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today, children will begin to:; Recall the letter sounds learned in Weeks 2 and 3.; Understand the big idea that separate sounds can be pushed together to make a word.; Break a simple spoken word into its separate sounds (segmenting).
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 5: Sight words — see, can, go (review & celebrate)
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today's lesson, children will:; Review all twelve sight words from the week.; Learn the final three sight words: see, can, go.; Do a gentle, comprehensive alphabet and phonics review.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 1: Review Letters A–Z
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today's lesson, children will:; Recognise all 26 letters of the alphabet, uppercase and lowercase.; Grow more confident naming letters through familiar games.; Practise forming a few letters with good pencil grip.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 2: Review Letter Sounds
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today's lesson, children will:; Say the sound for each of the 26 letters.; Tell apart sounds that are easy to confuse (p/b, t/d, m/n).; Hear and name the beginning sound in familiar words.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 3: Review Blending CVC Words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today's lesson, children will:; Push sounds together to read CVC words (like *cat, pen, sun*).; Read words using all five short vowels (a, e, i, o, u).; Build and change simple words with letter tiles.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 4: Review Sight Words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today's lesson, children will:; Recognise all 15 sight words quickly, on their own.; Read sight words inside simple sentences.; Match short sentences to pictures.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

liveSessions.title: Day 5: Celebration & End-of-Module Assessment
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare the low-cost materials listed in the source lesson plan.
liveSessions.agenda: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds · Year 1 · Ehel Academy · Teacher Lesson Plan; Daily Objectives; By the end of today's lesson, children will:; Show what they have learned across all four skills in a gentle, play-based assessment.; Celebrate their progress through Pre-Unit 0.; Receive a certificate of completion.
liveSessions.afterSession: Repeat one favourite sound, tracing or blending game for five minutes with an adult.

teacherNotes.note: This readiness module preserves the source sequence of five teacher-led lessons per week for six weeks. Repeat a day when needed; confidence and secure sound awareness matter more than speed.
Assess one-to-one through play, pointing, saying, tracing and blending. Do not turn the readiness check into a stressful test.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Answer: C. Day 1 gives Cat as the picture cue for the letter C. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Z, and the sound /z/. Day 5 covers letters U-Z and gives Zebra as the picture cue for Z. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Moon. The Day 5 sound focus is /m/ as in moon. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: k and c. Day 4 says that k and c share the /k/ sound. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: /n/. Day 1 lists /n/ as in nest. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: u. Day 2 says q almost always brings a u with it. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Sun. The objectives use sun as the example of blending /s/ /u/ /n/. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Hut. The word-building example in the objectives is cat, then hat, then hut. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I, a, the. Day 1 says: review alphabet and CVC, then learn I, a, the. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I see a cat. The objectives show 'I see a cat.' as the first tiny sentence. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: p and b. Day 2 says to practise telling p/b and t/d apart. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: 15. The Week 6 objectives say children read the 15 sight words taught in Week 5. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “A says /a/.”. Model answers: ant, apple, arm. Watch for: Many children say the letter name 'ay' instead of the sound /a/. Model the short /a/ first, then let them copy.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “M says /m/.”. Model answers: mum, mat, moon. Watch for: Some children add an extra sound and say 'muh'. Keep the lips shut and hum /m/ with no 'uh' at the end.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “S says /s/.”. Model answers: sun, sit, sand. Watch for: Children often say 'ess', the letter name. Hiss the sound instead, long and soft like a snake.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “c-a-t, cat.”. Model answers: c-a-t cat, m-a-t mat, s-a-t sat. Watch for: Some children say the letters' names (see-ay-tee) instead of their sounds (c-a-t). Say the sounds slowly, then blend.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I see a / an ___.”. Model answers: I see a book. I see an apple. I see a bag. Watch for: Children pop 'a' before apple by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an apple, an egg. Say it slowly together.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I can ___.”. Model answers: I can run. I can jump. I can read. Watch for: Some children say 'I can running.' After 'can' we use the plain action word: 'I can run.'
Success is four letters found and named: an A on a book, an M on a packet, an S on a sign, and the first letter of their own name. Accept pointing plus either the letter name or its sound.
The /s/ pile is sun, sock and seed; the /m/ pile is mat, moon and milk. Accept a correct sort even when the word is stretched out slowly with your help or the sound is whispered.
The three complete sets are A-a-apple, T-t-tap and P-p-pen. Accept a child who matches by picture first and then finds the letters, and count /a/, /t/ or /p/ as right if roughly formed.
Listen for sun, map, tin and bag blended into whole words. Accept a slow sound-by-sound blend, one prompt from you before the word arrives, and any brave robot voice when they take their own turn.
Look for snapping only on true pairs of I, see, a and can, followed by reading the word aloud. Accept a slow sound-out or the word echoed after you; who wins matters far less than the reading.
A correct build is any sensible order, such as 'I can see a cat', read left to right. Accept finger-pointing while reading, and 'I can see a sun' after the last card is swapped.
Correct option: A. Small a is written as capital A; B, D and O are the partners of b, d and o.
Correct option: m. Capital M is written as small m; w is m turned over, and n and s are different letters.
Correct option: ball. Ball starts with b, so we hear /b/; cat starts with /c/, sun with /s/ and fish with /f/.
Correct option: sun. Sun starts with s, so we hear /s/; moon, dog and hat begin with /m/, /d/ and /h/.
Correct option: cat. The middle sound is /a/ and the last sound is /t/, so c-a-t says cat, not cap or can.
Correct option: sun. The last sound is /n/, so s-u-n says sun; sum ends with /m/ and run begins with /r/.
Correct option: The. The is our sight word and it goes before cat; the other words are already used in the sentence.
Correct option: see. See is a doing word that fits after I; moon, hat and pen are naming words, so they do not work here.
Correct option: I see a cat.. Only this one starts with the capital letter I; the others start with small i, s and c.
Correct option: I can hop.. Only this one has the little dot at the end; the others have no full stop.

selfAssessment.statement: I can recognise and name uppercase and lowercase letters.

selfAssessment.statement: I can connect letters a-z with their most common sounds.

selfAssessment.statement: I can hear and say beginning sounds in familiar words.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can blend and read simple CVC words.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can recognise ten first sight words.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can build and read a tiny patterned sentence.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level1: Marks do not yet show the idea
rubrics.level3: Labels or copies a meaningful phrase
Uses a capital or end mark with support
rubrics.level4: Uses a capital and end mark independently
